actions.thought: Holding QJs on the button in a 3-handed game is a premium starting hand. I will open with a standard 3x raise to take initiative, capitalize on my positional advantage, and build the pot early.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (9-2 offsuit). Calling a raise from the button out of position with such a poor hand is negative expected value. Preserving my stack early in a tournament is critical, so folding is the disciplined and correct professional play.

We hold a middle pocket pair (77) in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x open from the Button. With effective stacks at nearly 100 BB, we have excellent implied odds to set-mine. Our hand also retains decent equity and showdown value on lower flops. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here, keeping the opponent's range wide while avoiding building a massive pot out of position against potentially stronger hands.

We flopped an underpair to the Ten, but an overpair to the 5 and 3. The board is somewhat coordinated with a heart flush draw and potential straight draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the raiser. This protects our checking range and allows us to evaluate their continuation bet sizing before making a decision.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in position on a Ten-high board with a heart draw. While I haven't hit the board directly with my Queen-Jack of clubs, I have two overcards and a backdoor club draw. Pro checked, which is expected. A standard continuation bet here exerts pressure on the Big Blind's range, which likely contains many missed hands or weak pairs that may fold to aggression. Betting 300 into a 650 pot is a consistent play to maintain initiative.

We have pocket sevens on a Th 5c 3h flop. The opponent's continuation bet of 300 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing. We are currently ahead of their missed overcards, flush draws, and straight draws, but we trail any Ten, overpairs, or sets. Raising here overplays our hand and isolates us against better holdings, while folding to a single bet with second pair against a wide Button range is too tight. Calling is the optimal play to control the pot size, keep the opponent's bluffing range wide, and evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings a Jack, which is an overcard to our pocket sevens and connects well with the pre-flop raiser's range. We are now holding third pair. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor to control the pot size and evaluate their action. Leading out makes little sense as we would only get called by better hands.

The turn Jack of Spades gives me top pair with a strong kicker (QJ). After Pro checked to me, I have a clear value betting opportunity. I want to extract value from hands like pocket pairs, a Ten, or heart draws that Pro likely called with on the flop. Betting also protects my hand from a potential heart or straight completing on the river. A bet of 700 into a 1250 pot (approximately 56%) is a standard size to build the pot while keeping Pro's continuing range wide.

The turn brings a Jack, introducing a second overcard to our pocket sevens. The opponent's double-barrel on this turn indicates continued strength, representing hands like a Jack, a Ten, overpairs, or strong semi-bluffs. With third pair out of position, our hand is now severely degraded in value and we lack the equity or positional advantage to continue calling sizable bets. We are beating very few hands other than pure bluffs, and even missed overcards or draws have decent equity against us. Folding is the most profitable and disciplined decision.

Holding Jack-Seven offsuit on the button is a weak starting hand in a 3-handed game. With 150 hands remaining in the tournament, it is strategically sound to conserve my stack and avoid playing speculative hands out of position or with low equity. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my chip count for more favorable situations.

With 43 suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a very marginal hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. While suited connectors have some playability, 43s is too weak and easily dominated to justify a limp or a raise from this position. The most disciplined and optimal action is to fold and preserve my stack.
52 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. From the button facing no action, it is still a clear fold.

With T6o in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, I have a decision between folding, completing, or raising. T6o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position if the Big Blind calls. Given it is early in the tournament and I want to avoid high-variance spots with marginal hands, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a three-handed game. While I have the advantage of position, J6o lacks both high-card strength and connectivity. Opening here would be a pure steal attempt, but given the hand's poor playability if called, I will choose the disciplined path and fold, waiting for a better opportunity to involve myself in a pot.

5h-9s is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. Even though the button has folded, being in the small blind against the big blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. With a stack of 9950, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand than to contest a small pot with a disadvantage.

Positioned on the button with Q5s in a 3-handed game, this is a strong candidate for an open-raise. My goal is to capture the blinds or secure the initiative to play in position. A raise to 300 is a standard size to test the responsiveness of my opponents.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, Queen-Ten offsuit plays poorly out of position in the Small Blind. Calling can trap us against the Big Blind or leave us dominated post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand isn't ideal compared to suited connectors or blockers. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

I have a strong pocket pair (9d 9c) in the Big Blind. The button (Flashlight) opened for 3x, and the Small Blind folded. In a 3-handed game, 99 is a very strong hand against a button opening range. I will 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand out of position. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the opener and define their hand range.

I opened with Q5s from the button, but faced a significant 3-bet from the big blind. Q5s is a marginal hand that does not perform well against a typical 3-betting range, which usually contains stronger broadway cards or pairs. Given the early stage of the tournament and the poor relative strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
With 4c 7d offsuit from the button, my hand is extremely weak and has no playable value, even being first to act. It's best to fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with AsTc. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to act after me. A10o is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to take control of the pot, build value, and potentially end the hand immediately by pressuring the Big Blind to fold.

In a blind-versus-blind scenario, my opponent (Flash) has raised from the small blind. Holding 8-7 offsuit, I have sufficient pot odds to defend my big blind. Since I am in the big blind, I will have position throughout the post-flop play, which makes calling 200 more to a 400 total pot a standard, profitable decision against a wide small blind opening range.

I have flopped Two Pair (Aces and Tens) on a very coordinated board of Qd-Ac-Td. While this is a strong hand, the board offers many potential draws, including a straight (any King or Jack) and a diamond flush. To extract value from weaker Aces or Queens and to charge opponents drawing to a straight or flush, I will lead with a standard value bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot.

The flop of Queen-Ace-Ten heavily favors the range of the small blind opener, and my hand (eight-high, no draw) has zero equity. Calling 400 into a 600 pot with essentially air against a range that hits this board consistently is a losing play. Folding is the only reasonable action here.

Jack-Six offsuit is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. While I have the chip lead and could attempt a blind steal from the button, J6o doesn't play well post-flop if called. Given that I've been active in previous hands, I'll take a disciplined approach and fold this marginal hand to maintain my stack and wait for a better opening.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. My hand, Ace-Six offsuit, is well above the range needed to attack the big blind. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard play here to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the 150 pot pre-flop or take the initiative into the flop.

2h 7h is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Even though it is suited and I would have position on the Small Blind post-flop, facing a 3x raise requires at least a decent playable hand. Folding is the only mathematically sound play here.

I am dealt six of spades and nine of diamonds, which is a very weak and unsuited starting hand. Even in a three-handed game on the button, this hand lacks the equity and playability to warrant an open-raise or a call of the big blind. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

With Q6s in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a decent suited hand to attempt a blind steal. Raising to 3 big blinds takes the initiative and puts pressure on the big blind, often winning the pot uncontested.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand, especially in a blind-vs-blind situation after the Button has folded. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I will 3-bet to 1000 to build value and take initiative in the hand, as my range is significantly stronger than a typical Small Blind opening range.

I attempted a blind steal with Q6s from the small blind, but Flash 3-bet me to 1000 from the big blind. Q6s plays poorly against a 3-bet range, especially out of position, and is easily dominated. Given the large sizing and being OOP, folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision.

Holding Th 7d on the Button. This is a very weak offsuit hand. Raising as a steal might work occasionally but Flash and Flashlight have been playing aggressively (Flash 3-bet to 1000 recently). Limping is not an option. The standard play is to fold.

As4s is a strong hand in a blind vs blind situation. Since the button folded, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. This hand has good equity and flush potential if we see a flop.

Facing a raise from the small blind while in the big blind, I have position for the remainder of the hand. With Qs3s, I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400 total), which justifies a call given the width of ranges in SB vs BB scenarios. I will see a flop and evaluate based on the texture.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain initiative, extract value from draws or high-card hands, and protect my current hand strength. If the opponent raises, I will likely have to fold, but a bet allows me to define where I stand in the hand.

The board texture (9-T-4 rainbow) connects reasonably well with the small blind's opening range. My hand, Qs-3s, offers only marginal backdoor flush potential and Q-high, which lacks the necessary equity to profitably call a half-pot continuation bet against a likely range. Folding is the most prudent and strategic decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

J7s is a standard open-raising hand from the button in a 3-handed game. Given that I have been successful with 3x opens in previous hands and both opponents have shown a tendency to play cautiously, raising to 300 is the most effective play to either win the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has decent equity and playability.

I am in the small blind holding 9h-3d, which is a very weak hand with no playability. Calling against a 3x open raise from the button (Flash) would be a significant mistake, as I would be out of position with a dominated hand. Folding is the standard and correct decision to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to defend or get involved. Folding is the only sensible choice.

Holding an Ace on the button provides strong equity to open-raise and attempt to steal the blinds. Ace-five is a standard hand to play from this position. Betting 300, which is 3x the big blind, is a standard sizing to either win the blinds immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Small Blind facing a Button open. This is a premium hand, and 3-betting is the standard and most profitable play here. By 3-betting to 1000, I can extract value from worse hands, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and take control of the hand going into the flop. Calling would invite the Big Blind into the pot and force me to play out of position in a multi-way pot.

I am holding Jack-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. Flashlight raised from the button and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. This hand is extremely weak and does not play well in multi-way or 3-bet pots. Given the strength shown by Pro's 3-bet, there is no reason to continue with this hand. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after opening on the button, I must weigh the pot odds and my positional advantage. While Ace-Five offsuit is not a premium hand, folding would be too exploitable given the current pot odds of 2:1. Calling allows me to play the hand in position, enabling me to better navigate post-flop streets and potentially outplay my opponent or hit a favorable board.

The flop comes 6c 4d 5s, which is heavily connected and hits my opponent's 3-bet calling range much better than my 3-betting range. Hands like medium pocket pairs (77-JJ) and suited connectors are well-represented in their range and connect strongly with this board. I only have Ace-King high with no immediate draws. Betting here would likely only get called or raised by better hands. The best play is to check and look to fold if faced with significant aggression, as my hand has very little equity on this texture.

The flop of 6c-4d-5s is highly connected and hits a 3-betting range hard, as it is likely to contain pocket pairs, suited connectors, and overpairs. My pair of fives with an Ace kicker is marginal and vulnerable on this board texture. Checking back is the optimal play to maintain pot control, avoid potential check-raises from a strong range, and reach showdown without bloating the pot with a hand that may already be behind.

The turn card is the 7c, putting four to a straight on the board (4-5-6-7). This board strongly favors my opponent's pre-flop calling range, which contains many medium pocket pairs and suited connectors that could easily have a straight, two pair, or a strong draw. With only Ace-high and no relevant draws, I have virtually zero equity against a calling range. Betting here as a bluff is extremely dangerous given the board texture. The most prudent action is to check and prepare to fold to any bet.

The board shows a straight (4-5-6-7), making my pair of fives highly vulnerable. Any opponent holding an 8 or a 3 currently has a straight, making my hand a low-equity bluff-catcher. Checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control, avoid being check-raised, and reach the river to showdown with minimal risk.

The river pairs the 6, leaving a board of 4-5-6-7-6. Flashlight checked both the flop and the turn, which indicates they likely have either a hand with marginal showdown value, such as a medium pocket pair, or unpaired broadway cards that missed. With Ace-King, I currently beat all other Ace-high and King-high hands (like AQ, AJ, KQ) that Flashlight might have in their range. Betting here as a bluff would only target pocket pairs like 99-JJ, which is risky and unnecessary given the strength of my hand against their missed high cards. Checking to realize my showdown value is the most profitable and lowest-variance play. If Flashlight bets, I will easily fold.

The board is highly coordinated (6-4-5-7-6), making a straight (3-x or 8-x) extremely likely for the opponent. My hand, two pair (sixes and fives) with an Ace kicker, is essentially a bluff-catcher. Given the opponent checked, they are either trapping with a very strong hand (like a full house or a straight) or have given up on the hand. Betting here provides little value as I will only be called by better hands, and checking behind allows me to reach showdown safely and collect the pot if my two pair is good.

I am dealt 4h 2c on the button, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I have no high card strength, no suitedness, and almost no straight potential. Even though I have position, attempting to steal the blinds with a hand this weak is not profitable in the long run. The standard and only correct play here is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o. The Button has folded. While I could attempt to steal the Big Blind, J3o is a very weak hand to play out of position if called. Given my chip lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with 7d 4h, which is a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, I should open a wide range from the button, but 74 offsuit is near the bottom of all possible hands. While I could attempt a steal, the risks of being re-raised or called and playing a weak hand out of position post-flop (if the small blind calls) or against a big blind defender make this a clear fold. I will wait for a more playable hand to get involved.

With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. While Queen-Six offsuit is a marginal holding, it is strong enough to play aggressively in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially steal the pot immediately, or at least gain the initiative for the hand.

3h 2s is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, there is absolutely no reason to defend or get involved. Folding is the only sensible and profitable play.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a playable hand to open from this position. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or take the initiative into the flop.

Q3o is a very weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind facing a raise from the button. The expectation of calling or 3-betting here is extremely negative. The optimal decision is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 6h, a very weak starting hand. Flashlight opened to 3x from the button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1 to call, 9-6 offsuit plays very poorly out of position and is well below the threshold for a standard defense against a button open. As the chip leader, I want to maintain my stack and look for more profitable spots rather than bleeding chips on marginal-to-poor holdings.

Playing 3-handed, J3 suited is a very marginal hand, even from the button. While open-raising as a steal is an option, both blinds have deeper stacks and have shown a willingness to 3-bet. The most solid and standard play is to fold and wait for better starting hands to preserve my stack.

I have Pocket Jacks, which is a premium starting hand. Since the button folded, it's just me and the Big Blind. Raising here is the standard play to build value and protect my hand, as JJ is significantly ahead of the Big Blind's random defensive range. I will raise to 300.
Flash is playing aggressively from the small blind. With 2-4 suited in the big blind, I have favorable pot odds to call and see a flop, as I am closing the action in a blind-versus-blind scenario.

I have flopped top set with my pocket Jacks on a J-T-3 rainbow board. This is an incredibly strong position. My opponent in the Big Blind likely has a range that includes many Tens, some Jacks (though I block most), and straight draws like KQ or Q9. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from these hands while keeping the pot growing.

The flop of 3s-Th-Jc completely missed my 2h-4h hand. I do not have a pair, a flush draw, or any reasonable straight draw. With no equity to justify calling a 300 chip continuation bet into a 900 chip pot, continuing would be negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

KJo is a strong hand to open with from the button in a three-handed game. My goal is to either take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a range advantage. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing that I have used successfully in previous hands.

Queen-Three offsuit is a weak hand that lacks connectivity and high-card strength. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive button raiser is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision to preserve my stack for stronger holdings.
J3s is a very weak suited hand that performs poorly out of position, even against a wide Button opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, and calling or raising would likely put me in a disadvantaged position against the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

A2o is a weak hand to play out of position. Flash has shown aggression in previous blind vs blind situations (e.g., 3-betting my SB open in Hand #8), so raising is risky, and limping exposes me to isolation raises. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

J8o is too weak of a holding to open from the button. Even though it's late position, playing a sub-par offsuit hand against deep-stacked opponents in the blinds is not profitable long-term. Folding is the optimal decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight) in the hand. In blind-vs-blind situations, K5o is strong enough to open for a steal or to play for value against a wider range. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four of clubs. The small blind has opened to 300. I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 400. Given the pot odds and that I am in position for the rest of the hand (as the BB), calling is mathematically sound and allows me to see a flop with a suited hand against an aggressive player.

The flop of 8c-9s-Tc is very coordinated and favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many middling connectors and suited hands. My hand, Kc-5h, has completely missed this board except for a very weak backdoor flush and straight possibilities. Betting into this board is risky as the opponent is likely to have a piece of it or a strong draw. I will check to see how the action develops.

The flop is 8c-9s-Tc, and I have a Jack-high flush draw. The pot is 600, and my opponent has checked to me. Since I am in position, checking back is the most optimal play to realize my equity for free and avoid getting check-raised on a highly coordinated board that hits my opponent's range. If I miss the turn, I can reassess, but for now, taking the free card keeps the pot size manageable and allows me to see if I can improve to a flush.

The turn card is a 3h, which is a complete blank and doesn't change the board state. On the flop, both of us checked. My King-high has very little showdown value on this connected board (8-9-10), and Flashlight's check-back on the flop doesn't necessarily mean they are weak, as many players will check back draws or medium strength hands on such a texture. I will check again to keep the pot small and avoid being check-raised or called while holding no equity.

I am on the turn with a Jack-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a seven). The pot is 600. Checking the flop allowed me to keep the pot manageable, but now that the turn has brought a relatively blank card (the 3h), I have significant equity with my combo draw. By betting here, I can either take down the pot immediately if my opponent is weak or build a larger pot for when I hit my draws on the river. A bet of 300 (half-pot) is a solid sizing to pressure the opponent while maintaining a good price to realize my equity.

On this highly coordinated board of 8-9-10-3, my King-high has virtually no equity and no draws to improve. I attempted to steal the blinds pre-flop and gave up on the flop when the board texture heavily favored the Big Blind's calling range. After checking twice, the opponent has bet half the pot. Given that almost any pair or even a straight draw in the opponent's range beats me, there is no reason to continue or attempt a bluff on a board that hits them so well.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand for three-handed play. I have the chip lead and should be opening frequently from this position to apply pressure to the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to either take the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

I am in the small blind with 8c5c. While suited, this is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an aggressive button opener. Calling would put me in a difficult post-flop situation without the betting lead, and 3-betting with such a weak holding is not advisable against an active player. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o, facing a button raise from Flash. J3o is a weak hand and unplayable against a standard 3x open, especially out of position. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct choice.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong holding in a three-handed game, particularly from the button position. I want to open the pot to take the initiative and pressure the small and big blinds. A raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size in this spot to define my range and attempt to win the blinds immediately or play a pot with position.

I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited low connector (5c6h). The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. 56o is far too weak to call or 3-bet from out of position, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. An easy fold.

I am holding a strong pocket pair (Ten of Hearts and Ten of Diamonds) in the Big Blind. Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 300, which is a standard opening. Pro in the Small Blind has folded. In a three-handed game, pocket Tens are very high in my range and significantly ahead of a typical button opening range. I want to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand, so I will 3-bet. A raise to 1000 total is a standard and effective size to put pressure on the button and potentially end the hand now or set up a post-flop advantage.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my initial open on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit. While AJo is a strong hand, 3-bets from the big blind in this format often represent a polarized or strong linear range. However, I have positional advantage, which is crucial in post-flop play. Folding is too conservative, and 4-betting here could bloat the pot unnecessarily with a hand that plays well enough in a single-raised or 3-bet pot. Calling 700 to contest a pot of 1350 gives me reasonable pot odds to see a flop and utilize my position.

I 3-bet pre-flop and the flop came with two Kings (Kc Kd 4c). My pocket Tens are a strong hand here, but the board is highly polarized. If Flashlight has a King, I am in trouble. If I bet, I mostly get called by better or fold out hands I already beat. By checking, I control the pot size, protect my checking range, and can see how Flashlight reacts to this dry board. It also gives Flashlight a chance to bluff with hands like AQ or AJ which I currently beat.

The board is heavily paired with a King, which significantly strengthens the range of my opponent (the 3-bettor). My Ace-High is currently a marginal bluff-catcher at best. Betting here into a 3-bet range is suboptimal as I will likely only be called by better hands or induce a check-raise. Checking behind allows me to maintain pot control and realize my position, effectively keeping the pot manageable while avoiding unnecessary bloating against a range that likely has the King or a pocket pair.

The turn card (7s) is a total brick and does not change the board texture significantly. Flashlight's check on the flop suggests they likely do not have a King, as most players would bet a King for value or protection on a 3-bet pot flop. My pocket Tens remain a very strong hand, likely ahead of Flashlight's range which consists of mid-pairs (88, 99), Ace-high hands, or club draws. I will bet 1000 into the 2050 pot to extract value from these hands and protect against potential draws. This sizing is large enough to charge draws while still being small enough to get called by hands I dominate.

I am facing a 1000 chip bet into a 2050 chip pot on a board of K-K-4-7. My opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has a range that heavily favors broadway cards and pocket pairs, all of which connect well with this King-paired board. Even though they checked the flop, their bet on the turn signals that they are likely turning a hand like a small pocket pair into a bluff or value betting a King. As I am holding Ace-high with no pair and no realistic draw, I am strictly a bluff-catcher. Calling with Ace-high here is mathematically unfavorable and risky, so the disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Pocket eights is a strong hand pre-flop, especially 3-handed. From the button, raising to 3x the big blind is standard to take control, build the pot, and extract value or take down the blinds immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. Pro has raised to 300 from the Button. K7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a Button raise, especially with a Big Blind still to act. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive, calling frequently leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am dominated or out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play here to avoid unnecessary chip loss with marginal holdings.

My hand, 4d 5c, is extremely weak and lacks playability. Even with the favorable pot odds offered by the button's raise, calling with this junk hand out of position is negative expected value. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am on the button with 6s 7h. While this hand has some connectivity, it is quite weak for an opening hand in a 3-handed game, especially offsuit. I have been very aggressive in previous hands, raising frequently from the button and small blind. To maintain a balanced image and avoid over-extending with marginal holdings when my opponents might be looking to play back at my high raise frequency, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 9s after the Button folded. This is a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. While 79o is a weak hand, the pot odds are favorable to complete the blind and see a flop. I will call the additional 50 chips to complete.

With Ah8s in the big blind facing a small blind limp, I have a strong hand that dominates many limping hands. Raising to 300 takes the initiative, builds the pot in position, and can win the pot immediately pre-flop.

7d 9s is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind against a raise. The risk-reward ratio does not justify continuing with this range, and calling would put me in a disadvantaged position post-flop. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Queen-five suited, which is a playable hand in late position. Given my position, opening the pot to steal the blinds is a standard strategy. I have been playing aggressively in previous hands, so a raise to 300 is consistent with my image and keeps pressure on the blinds.

I am dealt 72 offsuit in the Small Blind, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 300 from the Button, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand. Folding is the only correct decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace of Diamonds and Four of Hearts (A4o). The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. Pro (Small Blind) folded. It costs me 200 more to call into a pot that will be 650. In a 3-handed game, A4o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open. I have the chip lead and Flashlight has shown some tendency to fold to post-flop aggression in previous hands. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position with a marginal Ace.

I have Ace-high on a dry 9-5-2 rainbow board after defending my big blind. As the out-of-position defender, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. Leading here is unnecessary as my hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value or as a pure bluff.

I have middle pair (5s) with a Queen kicker on a dry, uncoordinated board. My hand is likely the best, but it is vulnerable to overcards like Ace-high or King-high, which are common in the Big Blind's calling range. Checking back could lose me the initiative and give free cards to my opponent. Betting 400 into the 650 pot is a standard continuation bet size that extracts value from worse hands and provides protection for my equity.

I defended the big blind with Ace-high and the flop is 9-5-2 rainbow, which is very dry. Flashlight is betting 400 into 650. In a three-handed dynamic, Ace-high often holds significant equity against a button opening range. While I am out of position, I have two overcards and the board texture is unlikely to have improved many hands in my opponent's range. I will call this continuation bet to see if the turn improves my hand or if my opponent checks back, allowing me to reach showdown or evaluate further.

On this turn (3c), I have completed a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5). My opponent, Flashlight, has shown aggression pre-flop and on the flop, suggesting they may have a mid-to-high pair or are continuing a semi-bluff. By checking, I allow them to continue their aggression and potentially commit more chips with a hand like top pair (9x) or an overpair. Leading out now would likely cause most of their bluffs to fold and only get called by hands that are currently drawing thin. I will check to induce a bet.

I have middle pair with a Queen kicker and a flush draw on a 9-5-2-3 board. The turn 3c gave me significant equity, making this a prime spot to bet for both value and protection. Since Flash checked, I want to take the initiative and potentially win the pot now or set up for a profitable river. A bet of 700 into a 1450 pot is well-sized to pressure marginal hands while building a pot for when I hit my flush.

I have turned an Ace-to-five straight (A-2-3-4-5) on a 9-5-2-3 board. Although Flashlight could potentially hold 6-4 for a higher straight, that is a narrow part of their range. Given their consistent aggression on both the flop and turn, they likely hold a strong top pair (9x), an overpair, or perhaps a set. I will raise here to extract value while the board is still relatively clean and to build the pot for a potential river shove, especially since a club or a six on the river could kill the action or allow my opponent to check behind.

I have middle pair (5s) with a Queen kicker and a flush draw on a 9-5-2-3 board. With 14 clean outs (9 clubs for the flush, 3 Queens for two pair, and 2 fives for trips), my equity is approximately 30%. Given that I need to call 1400 to win a total pot of 5650, the pot odds of roughly 25% make this a mathematically sound call. My hand has significant potential to improve, and calling allows me to realize that equity on the river.

On the river, the board is 9d-5h-2c-3c-Jc. I have a 5-high straight (A-2-3-4-5). While this was a very strong hand on the turn, the Jack of Clubs on the river is a significant scare card. It completes any potential backdoor flush (two clubs) and also completes a higher straight if Flashlight held 6-4. Given that Flashlight called my large turn raise, their range is narrowed to very strong hands like sets (99, 55, 22), two-pair, or draws that may have just arrived. If I bet, I risk being raised by a flush or a higher straight, and I'm unlikely to get called by many worse hands other than a set of 9s. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheaper showdown or catch a bluff if Flashlight was on a draw that missed (though many draws actually hit this river).

The river has completed the flush draw, and I hold the Queen of Clubs, giving me a Queen-high flush. Since my opponent, Flash, checked to me, they are likely either trapped with a weak hand or playing defensively. I have a strong made hand and should extract value. A bet of 2500 into the 5650 pot is a solid value bet that can get called by top-pair hands, two-pair combinations, or even lower flushes, while not over-committing if I am beat by a higher flush.

I have a five-high straight (A-2-3-4-5) on a board of 9-5-2-3-J. On the turn, I check-raised Flashlight's bet to 2100, and they called, indicating a very strong range such as a set, two-pair, or a straight/flush draw. The river Jack of Clubs is a concern because it completes a potential backdoor flush and a higher straight if the opponent held 6-4. However, the pot odds are roughly 3.2-to-1 (calling 2500 to win 8150 total), and in a three-handed game, a straight is too strong to fold to a single bet. Flashlight could be value-betting a set of 9s, 5s, or 2s, or perhaps a two-pair hand that they didn't want to fold on the turn. While I lose to 6-4 and flushes, my hand beats all sets and two-pairs, so I must call.

Q4s is a weak hand with limited playability and is easily dominated by better Queens. From the button, while I could open wide to steal the blinds, Q4s is generally a fold in this situation against opponents who have shown a willingness to 3-bet or call wide. I will preserve my stack and wait for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey suited (Ac 3c). Since the Button has folded, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Flashlight. A3s is a very strong hand for a heads-up pot. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure, as is standard play in this position.

I hold Ten-Three offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent. The pot odds do not justify a call, and defending with such a marginal holding against a raising range will lead to being dominated on most boards. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a strong starting hand for this position. I will put in a standard opening raise of 300 to apply pressure to the blinds and likely win the pot immediately or play a pot with position and a range advantage.

Qd 7d is a weak hand to defend out of position against an aggressive button raiser. With the big blind yet to act and the disadvantage of playing post-flop out of position, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack.

J4 offsuit is an extremely weak and disconnected hand. Despite getting somewhat reasonable pot odds to defend the big blind, the hand is completely unplayable against a standard button raise, and we would be out of position post-flop. Folding is the only correct decision here.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten suited, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Given my position and hand strength, I should open the action to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here.

My hand is 6s 7c, a weak offsuit connector. I am in the Small Blind facing a raise to 300 from the Button. Playing this hand out of position is highly unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind me. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight of Diamonds, a strong suited hand. Flashlight raised from the Button to 300. With 100 already posted, I only need to call 200 more to see a flop. I have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of King-Ten-Three. While top pair is a strong hand in a three-handed game, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight. I have middle pair with an Ace kicker on a King-high board with a potential flush draw. I have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of King-Ten-Three with two hearts. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a standard continuation bet of about half the pot. I have a full house (Kings full of Eights). The river card (King of Clubs) improved my two pair into a very strong hand. A bet of 800 into a 1350 pot is a standard value size that can be called by a wide range of second-best hands.

I am facing an 800 chip bet into a 1350 pot on the river with two pair (Kings and Tens with an Ace kicker). The board is King, King, Ten, Ten, Eight. I am in the Small Blind with 7c-9d, which is a weak, unsuited hand. Being out of position against the Big Blind, there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand, and folding is the most disciplined and conservative action to preserve my stack size.

The hand 4s 3d is a very weak, uncoordinated starting hand with essentially no showdown value. In a 50/100 No-Limit game, playing such marginal cards is negative expected value. 43 offsuit is a very weak hand, and playing it out of position against the Big Blind is a losing strategy. The best move is to fold.

With pocket sevens on the Button, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise for value and to thin the field. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the best play to either steal the blinds or play heads-up in position.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise to 300 chips to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I hold 42o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, I have absolutely no equity to defend. This is an extremely straightforward fold.

Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand and not profitable to play from the button. The standard move is to simply fold and wait for a better spot to attack the blinds.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (3h, 4c). With the button (Flash) folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. I am in the Button position with a very weak hand, Ten-Deuce offsuit. This hand has very poor playability and equity, especially against two opponents. Action folds to me in the small blind. Queen-Nine suited (Qc 9c) is a solid starting hand with good playability and flush potential. Given the opportunity to steal the blinds or take control of the hand going into the flop, I will open with a standard raise to 3 big blinds.

I am in the big blind with 3d 4c. The small blind (Pro) has raised to 300. The button has folded, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind. With Jack-Ten offsuit, I have a playable hand in a heads-up situation. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control into the flop.
Facing a raise from the small blind, my hand (6h 4s) is much too weak to defend, even with the positional advantage post-flop. Folding is the correct choice to preserve my stack.

With Jack-Ten offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong positional advantage. Opening the pot with a raise to 300 is a standard move to take control of the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or continue with initiative post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 3s, which is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the most profitable and standard play is to simply fold and wait for better opportunities.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker (Qd Jh) on a Q-9-5 rainbow board. Flashlight opened from the button and is now continuation-betting 350 into a 650 pot. Queen-Five offsuit is a weak holding, even in a three-handed game from the button. There is no significant advantage to playing this hand, so folding is the most disciplined and conservative approach to maintain my stack.

I have an overpair (Queens) on a fairly wet board (8d 5c 4c) that contains both flush and straight draws. I have bottom pair (fours) and a flush draw (J-high). On the river 6c, the board reads 8d-5c-4c-Kd-6c. I have a pair of fours (bottom pair). After calling a pre-flop raise and a flop bet, the action went check-check on the turn when a King appeared. On the Button with 8h7h, a solid suited connector. Opening to 3x (300) is standard to pressure the blinds, steal the pot pre-flop, or play a highly playable hand in position if called.

I have 8-high on a Kd-Kh-Jd-Ts-2d board. I have absolutely no showdown value and will lose to any pair, Ace-high, or Queen-high. Q6s is a marginal suited hand. While it can occasionally be used as a steal from the Button, folding is the standard, safe play, especially given our stack size and the risk of facing a 3-bet from the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 84offsuit. The action has folded to me. While I could complete the small blind for an additional 50 chips, 84offsuit is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. I have a healthy stack and prefer to wait for better spots rather than bloating pots with marginal holdings.

I am on the button with an Ace. In a three-handed game, any Ace is a strong opening candidate from the button to steal the blinds or play in position. I will stick to my standard open-raising size of 300 chips.
K8o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise. Folding is the most disciplined approach to preserve my stack.
I am in the Big Blind with T3o, a very weak unsuited hand. The Button (Flash) has raised to 300. There is no reason to defend this hand, so a fold is the best action.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. While this is not a premium hand, the cost to enter the pot is only 100 chips. Given my position, calling to see a cheap flop is a reasonable, low-risk move to potentially capitalize on a favorable board, although I will play cautiously if I do not hit.

With K6s in the small blind and a button limper, we are getting excellent pot odds to complete. Raising out of position with a marginal suited King could bloat the pot unnecessarily against potentially stronger hands, so calling to see a cheap flop is the most profitable play.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Since both the Button and the Small Blind have limped into the pot, I have the option to check and see a free flop. There is no strategic value in raising here with such a poor holding, so I will take the free look.

We flopped the second nut flush draw with an overcard in a 3-way limped pot. Leading out from the small blind takes the initiative, gives us fold equity against random overcards or missed hands, and builds the pot in case we hit our flush. If raised, we have plenty of equity to continue.

I have hit bottom pair (3) on a flop of Qh-3h-5d. The Small Blind (Pro) has led out with a small bet of 150 into a 300 pot. This is a very small sizing, often indicative of a feeler bet or a drawing hand. While my hand is weak, bottom pair is enough to peel one street given the excellent pot odds (I need to call 150 to win a total pot of 600, giving me 4:1). If I improve on the turn to two pair or trips, I could win a decent pot, and if the action gets heavy, I can easily fold later.

The flop of Qh 3h 5d does not connect with my Jack-Four offsuit, and I have no pair or significant draw. Faced with a bet from the small blind and a call from the big blind, my Jack-high hand has effectively zero showdown value. Investing additional chips here would be a mistake, as the board is relatively dry, and I am unlikely to improve to the best hand. Folding is the prudent and disciplined decision.

We made the second nut flush on the turn. Flash called our flop bet, indicating he has some piece of the board like a pair of Queens, a smaller flush draw, or a straight draw. Betting here is mandatory for value and to charge any single heart draws, especially the Ace of hearts if he doesn't already have the flush. A half-pot bet of 300 is a good size to get called by worse made hands or draws while building the pot for a river value bet.

On the turn, the 8 of hearts completes potential flushes and many draws. I am holding bottom pair (3s) with a weak kicker and no heart for a flush draw. Pro has fired a second barrel into the pot, which suggests either a made hand (like a Queen or a flush) or a strong semi-bluff. My pair of 3s has very little equity to improve to a winning hand and is likely already beat. Given the board texture and the betting action, it is best to fold and preserve my stack.
I am on the button with pocket nines, which is a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is the standard opening size and allows me to build the pot in position or steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with JcTc, a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300. With a stack of over 13,000 chips, I have plenty of room to maneuver. Calling is standard here to see a flop with a hand that has great playability and equity against a button opening range, while 3-betting is also a viable option to take control of the pot. Given the previous history where Pro has folded to 3-bets (e.g., Hand #84, Hand #111), a 3-bet to 1000 is a strong play to exert pressure and potentially take the pot down immediately, while still having a hand that can flop well if called.

I am holding 5h 4s in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. Facing an initial raise from the Button and a subsequent 3-bet from the Small Blind, continuing in this pot would be highly unprofitable. I am out of position and do not have the equity to justify calling or reraising. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and strategic decision.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind, pocket nines is a strong enough hand to continue, especially since we have position. While 4-betting is an option, it risks facing a 5-bet shove which would force us to fold our equity. Calling keeps his entire 3-betting range (including bluffs, which he might have a lot of given our frequent folds to his 3-bets recently) in the pot and allows us to play post-flop in position with a strong pair or try to hit a set.

I 3-bet pre-flop and represent a strong range including overpairs and top pair with a good kicker. The flop (Qd 3s 6h) is relatively dry and favors my perceived range more than the caller's range. I will lead out with a continuation bet of approximately 1/3 pot to apply pressure and take down the pot now, or set up a barrel on later streets.

With pocket nines on a Qd-3s-6h board, we have second pair. Flash's 1/3 pot c-bet of 700 into 2100 is very standard and he will be doing this with his entire 3-betting range, including many overcards like AK, AJ, and smaller pairs or bluffs. Given the excellent pot odds (4:1) and our positional advantage, folding here is too weak. Calling allows us to keep his bluffs in and see how the turn develops.

I am in a 3-bet pot and have already followed up with a continuation bet on the flop. The turn 7s is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it could potentially hit a narrow part of a calling range (like 67s or 77). My Jack-high has no real showdown value and very little equity if called. While I considered a second barrel to push off middle pairs (88-TT), Pro's call on the flop suggests he may have a Queen or is determined to see a river. Checking here allows me to see how Pro reacts. If he checks back, I get a free river and potentially a chance to bluff or hit a Jack/Ten; if he bets, I can safely fold knowing I have zero equity against his value range.

With pocket nines on a Qd-3s-6h-7s board, my hand has decent showdown value but is essentially a bluff catcher or marginal made hand. Flash's turn check often signifies slowing down with overcards like AK/AJ or pot-controlling a medium-strength hand like TT-JJ or a weak Queen. If I bet, I am likely only called by better hands (like a Queen or higher pocket pair) or strong draws, and I risk getting check-raised all-in, which would force me to fold my equity. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and navigate the river with position.

On the river, the board pairs the trey (Qd 3s 6h 7s 3h). My hand, Jack-high, has no showdown value and loses to any pair. In the previous street (turn), both I and the opponent (Pro) checked, which often indicates marginal strength or a desire to reach showdown cheaply. Pro's range likely includes medium pairs like 88-JJ or a weak Queen. Given that I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, checking the turn makes my range look capped or like a surrender. A river bluff is unlikely to push Pro off a hand like pocket tens or a Queen, especially since the pot is already sizable (3500) and Pro's remaining stack is 5700. I will check and hope for a check-back to see if my Jack-high somehow wins, but I am prepared to fold to a bet.

With pocket nines on a Qd-3s-6h-7s-3h board, I have solid showdown value. Flash has shown weakness by checking both the turn and the river, indicating a range heavily weighted toward missed overcards like AK or AJ, which I currently beat. If I bet, better hands like TT, JJ, or a slow-played Queen will call or raise, while worse hands will simply fold. I have no value to extract and no reason to turn my hand into a bluff. Checking behind guarantees a safe showdown to win the pot against his missed broadways.

I am on the button with 9c 4c, a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, 9-high trash is rarely worth playing, even from the button. There is no need to bloat the pot or attempt a steal here when my stack is healthy and I can wait for better spots to utilize my position. I will simply fold and move on to the next hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 4d, which is a very weak hand. Since Flash folded, I only have to face Pro (the Big Blind). Given the weakness of my hand and the lack of incentive to play it out of position against the Big Blind, folding is the most disciplined and optimal strategy to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with a suited Ace (Ah6h). This is a strong enough hand to play in position. Since there has been no raise yet, calling the 100 chips to see a flop in position is a sound strategy.

I am dealt 74o in the small blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even with the button limping and getting 5:1 odds to call, playing this out of position against two opponents is a losing play. An easy fold.

I have been dealt pocket Queens (Qd, Qh) in the big blind. This is a premium hand. Flashlight, on the button, has limped in for 100, and Pro has folded from the small blind. There are 250 chips in the pot, and the action is on me. I need to build the pot and not let Flashlight see a cheap flop with a potentially weak range. I will raise to 400 total to value bet my hand and isolate the action.

I am on the button holding suited Ace-six (Ah6h). After limping for 100, the Big Blind raised to 400. This is a very standard situation where my suited Ace has good post-flop equity and position. Folding is too conservative given the pot odds, and 3-betting might inflate the pot too much against an aggressive player. Calling allows me to take the hand to a flop in position and evaluate based on the texture.

I have an overpair (Queens) on a low, scattered board of 5-9-2 with a spade flush draw. My opponent limped pre-flop and then called my raise, which often indicates a range of middle pairs, suited connectors, or high cards. I am ahead of most of that range. I will place a continuation bet of 400 into the 850 pot to extract value from hands like top pair (9x), smaller pocket pairs, or flush draws, and to protect my equity against potential draws.

I am holding a flush draw with the Ace of hearts, and there is one heart on the flop. The pot is 1250 and the opponent has bet 400. I have pot odds of approximately 1:3, which easily justifies a call given my equity with the flush draw and the potential for an Ace-high pair to be the best hand later. Raising is unnecessary and aggressive, so calling is the optimal path to see the turn.

The turn card, the 4 of spades, is a significant board change. It completes a three-flush (9s, 2s, 4s) and adds potential straight draws (A3, 63). Since I hold the Qd and Qh, I do not have a spade for protection or a redraw. My opponent's pre-flop limp-call followed by a call on the flop is very consistent with a range containing suited cards, specifically spade draws or middle-pair hands. If I bet now, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I would be in a difficult position if raised. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Flashlight reacts to the flush-completing card. If Flashlight checks back, I can reassess on the river. If they bet, I can decide whether to call based on the bet sizing and my read on their aggression.

I am in position on the button with the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3 for a straight) on a board of 5h, 9s, 2s, 4s. The opponent (Flash) has checked the turn. Checking behind is the optimal play here; it allows me to realize my equity for free and take a river card without committing more chips into the pot, maintaining pot control while still having a significant chance to improve to the best hand on the river.

The board finished with three spades and some minor straight possibilities (68, 36). My pocket Queens remain an overpair to the board. Flashlight's check on the turn after the third spade arrived is a strong indicator that they likely do not have a flush, as a made flush would typically bet for value and protection there. By checking the river, I am playing it safe against the small part of their range that might have slow-played a monster, while also inducing potential bluffs from missed straight draws or high-card hands that might try to steal the pot. If Flashlight checks back, I expect my Queens to be the best hand the vast majority of the time. If they bet, I will likely call unless the sizing is extremely large, as their turn check weakened their perceived range significantly.

I am on the button with Ace-high and the board (5h, 9s, 2s, 4s, 7h) offers no pair for my hand. My opponent, Flash, has checked the river, signaling potential weakness or a desire to check-fold. Given that I have no pair, betting for value is not viable, and bluffing against an aggressive opponent is high-risk. Checking behind is the optimal strategic decision to minimize losses and reach a showdown, where my Ace-high might still win if Flash is also playing a missed draw.

On the Button with 9d7d in a 3-handed game, this is a strong enough suited gapper to open-raise. I'll make a standard raise to 300 to pressure the blinds, steal the dead money, and take control of the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind holding Qd-Tc. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 300. I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to win 450) to make the call.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d-Th, a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand. While the pot odds (3.5-to-1) technically offer a decent price to call, playing a weak hand like 6-10 offsuit out of position against two aggressive opponents is a negative expected value play in the long run. On this monotone heart flop (Jh 6h 3h), I have the Qd and Tc. The Qd is the second-best heart, giving me a strong draw to the near-nuts if another heart arrives. If they bet, I can evaluate based on the size, but with the Qd blocker/draw, I am likely to at least call one bet.

With 9-high and no heart on a monotone heart board, I have essentially zero equity and no draws. A continuation bet here is unlikely to succeed often enough against a Small Blind calling range, which will frequently contain at least one heart, a pair, or both. The safest and most standard play is to check behind and be prepared to give up the hand.

On the turn 5s, the board remains Jh 6h 3h 5s. I misidentified my hand in previous reasoning; I actually hold Qd Tc, meaning I have no hearts and thus no flush draw on this monotone board. Pro checked back on the flop, which could indicate a lack of a made flush, but also suggests they might have a marginal made hand or a high heart they are playing cautiously. Since I have zero equity and no draw, betting would be a pure bluff into a range that likely contains at least some showdown value or draws that won't fold. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as I have no way to win the pot currently other than by my opponents folding.

With 9-high on a monotone board and no draws, I still have essentially zero equity. While my opponent has checked twice, signaling weakness, they could still easily have a single big heart (like an Ace or King) or a small pair that will call a delayed continuation bet to see the river. Checking behind is the safest option, maintaining pot control and allowing me to evaluate on the river if a bluff is profitable or if I should just surrender the hand.

On this river, an 8 of hearts has appeared, making a four-flush on the board (Jh 6h 3h 5s 8h). I hold Qd Tc, meaning I have no hearts and zero showdown value; I am literally playing the board's flush if it were to improve, but currently, any single heart in Pro's hand beats me. Pro has checked back both the flop and the turn, which might suggest they lack a strong flush, but they could easily have a marginal pair or a low heart that they were happy to take to showdown. Since I have no way to win this pot at showdown and bluffing into a four-flush board is extremely risky (as even the smallest heart will call), the most prudent move is to check. If Pro bets, I will fold, as I have no equity to continue.

With the board completing a 4-flush on the river and me holding just 9-high with no heart, I have essentially zero showdown value and will lose to any pair or Ace/King high. However, Flash has checked all three streets, strongly indicating they do not have a heart or any hand they feel comfortable calling with on a 4-flush board. This is a perfect spot to bluff, as my opponent's range is heavily capped and weak. A bet of around half-pot to two-thirds pot will credibly represent a flush and put maximum pressure on any of their non-heart hands, generating a very high fold equity.

On the river, the board has four hearts (Jh 6h 3h 8h). I hold the Qd and Tc, meaning I have no hearts and therefore no flush. My hand is simply Queen-high. Pro checked the flop and turn, but has now bet 400 into a 700 pot on the river. Since there are four hearts on the board, any player holding a single heart of any rank has a flush. Even if Pro does not have a heart, they likely have a pair or an Ace-high/King-high hand that beats my Queen-high. I have zero showdown value and no reason to believe a bluff-catch would be successful here. Folding is the only logical play.

I am currently the chip leader with a stack of 12,275. Hand #121 is on the Button and I have 7h 6s. I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind (Pro). With King-Ten offsuit, I have a decent hand for heads-up play. Given my stack size and the desire to remain aggressive in the late stages of the tournament, I will raise to 300 to challenge the big blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

T8 suited is a strong enough hand to defend my Big Blind against a Small Blind open. Calling is standard here to play a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop playability and straight/flush potential.

The flop is paired (9c-9s-7c), which is a dry board that does not hit my King-Ten offsuit. I have minimal equity with just King-high and no strong draws. I have an open-ended straight draw on a paired board. Since my opponent checked, betting half pot applies pressure to fold out overcards while building the pot in case I hit my straight.

The flop is paired (9s-9d-7c), and my King-Ten offsuit has completely missed, leaving me with only King-high. Pro has shown strength by betting into the pot on this dry texture. Calling with no pair and no realistic chance of improvement against a range that likely connects with this board is mathematically unfavorable. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

Kd Td is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Given my position on the button and no prior action, raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and optimal play to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds.

Facing a Button raise from Flashlight, ATo is a strong enough hand to defend from the Small Blind. I am holding 3h 4c in the Big Blind. Flashlight raised from the button and Pro 3-bet from the Small Blind. This hand has almost zero equity against the ranges of an opener and a 3-better. Given the strength shown by both opponents and my extremely poor starting hand, there is no reason to commit any chips to this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K-Ts is a strong suited hand that plays well in position. The 3-bet from Pro is aggressive, but calling is standard given the positional advantage and the stack-to-pot ratio. I have sufficient equity to see a flop and evaluate my position post-flop.

I completely missed the flop, having only Ace-high with no strong draws on a coordinated board (Qc 5s 4s). C-betting here with AdTc is a low-equity bluff, as Flashlight's 3-bet calling range contains plenty of Qx hands, pocket pairs, and flush draws that will not fold to a single bet. Checking to exercise pot control and likely giving up to a strong bet is the most prudent play out of position.

Pro checked to me in a 3-bet pot on a Q-5-4 board. My hand (K-T offsuit) is essentially high-card air with no significant equity or draws on this board texture. Betting into the 3-bettor (Pro) here is unlikely to force a fold from any better hand, and it would bloat the pot if I am behind. Checking back is the standard, conservative play to realize my equity and see a free turn card while keeping the pot controlled.

With Ace-Ten offsuit on a Qc 5s 4s 5d board, we still only have Ace-high. The flop checked through, meaning Flashlight's range is heavily weighted toward marginal made hands like medium pocket pairs, weak Queens, or various draws, as well as some high-card misses. Betting the turn as a bluff might fold out some of his air, but it's unlikely to fold out his pairs or strong draws. Since our Ace-high retains some showdown value against his missed draws and complete air, checking is the safest line. It controls the pot size and allows us to comfortably fold if he starts firing, or potentially reach a cheap showdown if he checks back again.

The opponent (Pro) has checked on the turn, and the pot is a 3-bet pot. My K-high has almost no showdown value on a board of Qc-5s-4s-5d, and betting here as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from the 3-bettor. Checking back is the optimal strategy to keep the pot controlled and reach the river cheaply, allowing me to potentially realize my equity if I improve or simply give up if the river doesn't help.

The river completes the flush draw with the 8 of Spades. We still only have Ace-high and hold no Spades to block the flush. After the flop and turn checked through, Flashlight's range contains many weak made hands like medium pocket pairs (66-JJ) or paired Queens, as well as some high-card floats that may have backed into a flush. Since we lack a credible bluffing hand or blockers, turning our Ace-high into a bluff is unnecessarily risky. Checking allows us to safely fold if Flashlight bets his made hands or flushes, and gives us a chance to win at showdown if he checks back with worse Ace-highs or missed draws.

I am at the river with King-high on a board of Qc-5s-4s-5d-8s. My opponent (Pro) checked to me. Given that Pro 3-bet pre-flop, their range is skewed towards high cards and pocket pairs. My hand has very little showdown value, and any bet I make as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from the range of hands that beat me. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free and minimizes my losses if I am behind, while potentially winning the pot if Pro is also holding high-card air.
With A6o on the button in a 3-handed game, raising is the standard aggressive play to try to steal the blinds. We have an Ace blocker and position. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300).

I am holding 4h 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Pro raised to 300 from the button, and I am in the small blind. Even though the price to call is 250 more, this hand has very poor playability and equity against almost any range. Being out of position for the rest of the hand makes calling even less attractive. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I hold pocket Kings, a premium hand that necessitates aggressive play to extract value and take initiative. Pro raised from the button, and since I am in the big blind, a 3-bet is the standard strategic move to isolate them and build the pot.

I opened A6o on the button as a standard steal attempt, but Flashlight 3-bets from the big blind. A6 offsuit is dominated by most of his 3-betting range (stronger Aces, big pairs) and plays very poorly post-flop. We do not have the equity to call, and 4-bet bluffing with an offsuit Ace of this rank is suboptimal. Folding is the correct, disciplined play.

I am on the button with 3h 2c, which is the weakest starting hand in poker. Given my current stack size and the remaining hands in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. My cards, Ten-Nine offsuit, are reasonably playable in a heads-up situation. Completing the small blind to see a cheap flop is a standard, low-variance approach, especially given the current pot size and stack depths. I will call to complete the big blind.

With 95o in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limps, I have a very weak hand with no incentive to build a pot pre-flop. Checking allows me to see a free flop and evaluate from there without risking additional chips.

I have middle pair with a Ten kicker on a semi-coordinated board (7d 5d 9s). Checking is the standard play here as the small blind, allowing me to control the pot size and see how the big blind proceeds on this wet board. It keeps the pot manageable and prevents me from overcommitting with a medium-strength hand.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair on a draw-heavy board (7d 5d 9s). Since the Small Blind checked, I need to bet to protect my hand against flush and straight draws, as well as extract value from worse made hands like top pair or middle pair. A sizing of 150 builds the pot and properly charges drawing hands.

I have middle pair (Nines) on a relatively wet, coordinated board (7d 5d 9s). My opponent, the big blind, is betting 150 into a 200 pot. Folding is too weak, as my pair is likely the best hand currently against many of the big blind's potential bluffs, straight draws, or weaker pair holdings. Raising might isolate me against hands that have me beat or have significant equity against me. Calling is the most balanced approach, allowing me to keep the pot manageable, keep the opponent's bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn based on the runout.

The turn card (3s) is a relatively safe card that does not change the strength of my middle pair. As I am out of position, checking is the best play to control the pot size and avoid inflating it with a hand that is not strong enough to value bet. I will check to see the opponent's action on the river.

I still hold top and bottom two pair on a very draw-heavy board (diamonds, spades, and various straight draws). The 3s on the turn completes a rare 64 straight and brings in a backdoor flush draw, but I remain ahead of the vast majority of Flashlight's check-calling range, which mostly consists of top pairs, middle pairs, and combo draws. I need to continue betting for value and to heavily charge all of the drawing hands.

My pair of nines with a ten kicker remains a solid hand on this board (7d 5d 9s 3s). The turn bet of 350 into a pot of 850 is a reasonable sizing that could be a continuation bet with a weaker pair, a semi-bluff with a flush or straight draw, or a value bet. Folding would be too passive given my hand's strength and the board's texture, while raising risks bloating the pot against a potentially better hand or folding out bluffs. Calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and see the river.

The board has become extremely coordinated (5d 7d 8s 9s 3s), completing a straight for any player holding a 6 or a 4. My pair of nines is now a marginal holding, and I have very little showdown value if my opponent has any piece of this board. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size, avoid bloating the pot against a potentially better hand, and see a cheap showdown if the opponent checks behind.

The river brings the 8 of spades, completing potential straights for any 6 and a backdoor spade flush. My top and bottom two pair is now extremely vulnerable. The opponent's check-calling range on the flop and turn includes many hands that have now improved to beat me, such as 98, any 6, or runner-runner spades. Worse hands like a bare 9 or missed diamond draws are unlikely to call a river bet, making a value bet too thin and risking a check-raise from a better hand. Checking behind to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very marginal hand, even for the button position. With 25 hands left in the tournament, I should focus on playing more disciplined hands rather than speculative ones, especially when the hand offers little post-flop potential. Folding is the most conservative and strategic decision here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o, which is a very weak offsuit hand. Flashlight folded on the button. Playing J5o out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable in the long run, so I will fold.
With 76 offsuit from the button, my hand is too weak to justify opening or calling, even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the most standard and solid approach to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 3h. After the Button (Pro) folded, I am left in a blind-vs-blind situation against Flashlight. While 93s has some flush potential, it is a very weak hand overall. I could complete the blind for 50 more, but 93s is difficult to play out of position and usually results in losing more chips post-flop. I will fold to maintain my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with K3o. While it's a weak King, in a three-handed game, any King is often strong enough to open from the button to steal the blinds or take control of the pot. The blinds are 50/100, and it's 100 to me to call. Standard opening strategy from the button is to raise to 3x the big blind (300). This puts pressure on both Flashlight and Pro.
